---
title: "ML & Interpretability Coding Project"
subtitle: "From Neurons to Alignment: The Complete Pipeline"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    geometry:
      - margin=0.85in
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
    number-sections: false
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{amssymb}
      \usepackage{amsmath}
      \usepackage{tikz}
      \usepackage{enumitem}
      \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
      \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=1.5em}
      \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
---

\begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Time estimate} & 10--14 hours (split across multiple sessions) \\
\textbf{Language} & Python (PyTorch + TransformerLens) \\
\textbf{Output} & Working transformer + interpretability analysis + alignment investigation \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{1em}

This project integrates everything from Phase 4: you build a transformer from scratch, train it on a task with a known algorithmic solution, reverse-engineer the learned circuits, and investigate whether alignment-relevant properties can be detected mechanistically.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  every node/.style={font=\small},
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, minimum width=2.8cm, minimum height=0.9cm, align=center, fill=white},
]
  \node[box, fill=blue!8] (build) {Part 1\\Build};
  \node[box, fill=green!8, right=1.2cm of build] (train) {Part 2\\Train};
  \node[box, fill=orange!8, right=1.2cm of train] (interp) {Part 3\\Interpret};
  \node[box, fill=red!8, right=1.2cm of interp] (align) {Part 4\\Align};

  \draw[->, thick] (build) -- (train);
  \draw[->, thick] (train) -- (interp);
  \draw[->, thick] (interp) -- (align);

  \node[below=0.3cm of build, font=\footnotesize, text=gray] {Lessons 53--60};
  \node[below=0.3cm of train, font=\footnotesize, text=gray] {Lessons 54--58};
  \node[below=0.3cm of interp, font=\footnotesize, text=gray] {Lessons 61, 65--66};
  \node[below=0.3cm of align, font=\footnotesize, text=gray] {Lessons 63--64};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\newpage

# Part 1 --- Build a Transformer from Scratch (\~3h)

**Goal:** Implement a small GPT-style transformer in PyTorch, without using any pre-built transformer modules.

## 1a. Token embedding and positional encoding

- Implement `nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)` for token embeddings
- Implement learned positional embeddings: `nn.Embedding(max_seq_len, d_model)`
- The input to the first transformer block is the sum of both

## 1b. Single attention head

Implement `CausalSelfAttention`:
\begin{align}
Q &= XW_Q, \quad K = XW_K, \quad V = XW_V \\[4pt]
\text{scores} &= \frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} + \text{causal\_mask} \\[4pt]
\text{attn} &= \text{softmax}(\text{scores}) \cdot V
\end{align}

The causal mask sets all entries above the diagonal to $-\infty$ before softmax.

## 1c. Multi-head attention

Split $d_\text{model}$ into $n_\text{heads}$ parallel heads, each with $d_k = d_\text{model} / n_\text{heads}$. Concatenate outputs and project through $W_O$.

## 1d. Transformer block

Stack: LayerNorm $\to$ Multi-Head Attention $\to$ Residual $\to$ LayerNorm $\to$ MLP $\to$ Residual.

The MLP is: $\text{Linear}(d_\text{model}, 4 \cdot d_\text{model}) \to \text{GELU} \to \text{Linear}(4 \cdot d_\text{model}, d_\text{model})$.

## 1e. Full GPT model

Stack $n_\text{layers}$ transformer blocks. Final layer: LayerNorm $\to$ Linear($d_\text{model}$, vocab\_size) $\to$ logits.

**Architecture for this project:**

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ll}
\toprule
\texttt{n\_layers} & 2 \\
\texttt{n\_heads} & 4 \\
\texttt{d\_model} & 128 \\
\texttt{d\_mlp} & 512 \\
\texttt{max\_seq\_len} & 6 \\
\texttt{vocab\_size} & task-dependent \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Keep it small --- you need to be able to train it on a laptop and interpret every component.

\newpage

# Part 2 --- Train on an Algorithmic Task (\~2h)

**Goal:** Train on a task with a known ground-truth algorithm, so you can verify your interpretability findings against the true solution.

## 2a. The task: modular addition

Given two numbers $a, b \in \{0, 1, \ldots, p-1\}$ where $p = 113$ (prime), predict $(a + b) \bmod p$.

**Input format:** three tokens --- $[a, b, =]$ where $=$ is a special token.

**Output:** the model predicts the answer token at the position after $=$.

This task is ideal because:
\begin{itemize}
  \item The ground-truth algorithm is known (addition mod $p$)
  \item It exhibits \textbf{grokking} --- the model memorizes first, then suddenly generalizes
  \item The learned circuit (Fourier features + trig identity) is well-understood in the literature
\end{itemize}

## 2b. Dataset

- Total examples: $p^2 = 12{,}769$ pairs
- Train/test split: 30\% train, 70\% test (small training set forces generalization)
- Shuffle training data each epoch

## 2c. Training

- Optimizer: AdamW with weight decay $= 1.0$ (high weight decay is critical for grokking)
- Learning rate: $10^{-3}$
- Train for 20{,}000+ epochs (grokking typically happens between 5{,}000--15{,}000 epochs)
- Log training loss, test loss, and training/test accuracy every 100 epochs

## 2d. Observe grokking

Plot training and test accuracy vs.\ epoch. You should see:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Training accuracy hits 100\% quickly ($\sim$500 epochs) --- the model \textbf{memorizes}
  \item Test accuracy stays near chance ($\sim$1/113) for thousands more epochs
  \item Test accuracy suddenly jumps to $\sim$100\% --- the model \textbf{grokks}
\end{enumerate}

Save model checkpoints at: (a) before memorization, (b) after memorization but before grokking, (c) after grokking. You'll analyze all three in Part 3.

\textit{Save as} \texttt{grokking\_curves.png}\textit{.}

\newpage

# Part 3 --- Mechanistic Interpretability (\~3h)

**Goal:** Reverse-engineer the algorithm the model learned after grokking.

## 3a. Load into TransformerLens

Convert your trained model weights into a \texttt{HookedTransformer} so you can use TransformerLens's analysis tools. Alternatively, add hooks to your own model to extract activations.

## 3b. Attention pattern analysis

For 50 random inputs, extract and average the attention patterns from each head. Plot as heatmaps.

After grokking, you should see structured patterns:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Some heads attend from the $=$ position back to $a$ (reading the first operand)
  \item Some heads attend from $=$ back to $b$ (reading the second operand)
  \item The pattern should be crisp and consistent across inputs
\end{itemize}

Compare attention patterns \textbf{before} and \textbf{after} grokking. Before grokking (memorization phase), the patterns should be messy and input-dependent. After grokking, they should be clean and algorithmic.

\textit{Save as} \texttt{attention\_before\_after.png}\textit{.}

## 3c. Embedding analysis: Fourier features

The known solution to modular addition uses Fourier features. After grokking, the embeddings for numbers $0, 1, \ldots, p-1$ should encode $\sin(2\pi k n / p)$ and $\cos(2\pi k n / p)$ for several frequencies $k$.

- Extract the embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times d_\text{model}}$
- Compute the discrete Fourier transform of each embedding dimension across the $p$ tokens
- Plot the power spectrum --- you should see sharp peaks at specific frequencies

\textit{Save as} \texttt{fourier\_features.png}\textit{.}

## 3d. Direct logit attribution

Decompose the output logits into contributions from:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Each attention head's output (via the OV circuit)
  \item Each MLP layer
  \item The token embeddings directly
\end{itemize}

Identify which components contribute most to the correct answer. After grokking, most of the ``work'' should be concentrated in 1--2 heads.

## 3e. Ablation study

For each attention head, zero out its output and measure the drop in test accuracy. Heads that cause a large drop when ablated are essential to the circuit. Heads with no effect are irrelevant.

Create a table:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
\textbf{Head} & \textbf{Accuracy (intact)} & \textbf{Accuracy (ablated)} \\
\midrule
L0H0 & 99.8\% & ??? \\
L0H1 & 99.8\% & ??? \\
\ldots & & \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\newpage

# Part 4 --- Alignment Investigation (\~3h)

**Goal:** Use your interpretability tools to investigate alignment-relevant questions.

## 4a. Can you detect memorization vs.\ generalization mechanistically?

You saved checkpoints at three stages: pre-memorization, post-memorization, post-grokking.

For each checkpoint, compute:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Attention pattern entropy (are patterns sharp or diffuse?)
  \item Embedding Fourier spectrum (are there clean frequency peaks?)
  \item Weight norm per component
\end{itemize}

Create a figure showing how these metrics evolve across the three phases. The transition from memorization to generalization should be visible in the internal structure, not just the loss curve.

\textit{Save as} \texttt{phase\_transition\_internals.png}\textit{.}

## 4b. Targeted capability removal

Pick one frequency $k$ that your Fourier analysis identified as important. Surgically ablate it:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Project the embeddings onto the $\sin(2\pi k n/p)$ and $\cos(2\pi k n/p)$ directions
  \item Zero out those components
  \item Measure accuracy on the full test set
\end{itemize}

The accuracy should drop significantly for inputs where this frequency matters, but remain high for others. This demonstrates \textbf{surgical capability removal} --- a key alignment technique (removing dangerous capabilities while preserving useful ones).

## 4c. Reward hacking simulation

Modify the training objective slightly: instead of predicting $(a + b) \bmod p$, add a ``reward bonus'' for outputting a specific wrong answer (e.g., always outputting 0 gets a small reward bonus).

Train from the grokked checkpoint with this modified objective. Observe:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Does the model ``hack'' the reward (start outputting 0 more often)?
  \item How quickly does it abandon the correct algorithm?
  \item Can you detect the reward hacking mechanistically (changes in attention patterns, embedding structure)?
\end{itemize}

This is a miniature version of the RLHF reward hacking problem.

## 4d. Write-up: connecting interpretability to alignment (\~500 words)

Write a short essay answering:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item What did mechanistic analysis reveal that loss curves alone could not?
  \item Could you detect the memorization $\to$ generalization transition from internal structure before seeing the test accuracy jump?
  \item How would surgical ablation (Part 4b) apply to a real alignment scenario (e.g., removing a model's ability to generate harmful content while preserving helpfulness)?
  \item What are the limitations of this approach for frontier models?
\end{enumerate}

\newpage

# Deliverables

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Working transformer} that trains to 100\% accuracy on modular addition via grokking
  \item \texttt{grokking\_curves.png} --- training/test accuracy showing the grokking phenomenon
  \item \texttt{attention\_before\_after.png} --- attention patterns pre- and post-grokking
  \item \texttt{fourier\_features.png} --- embedding Fourier spectrum showing learned frequencies
  \item Ablation table identifying the essential circuit components
  \item \texttt{phase\_transition\_internals.png} --- mechanistic metrics across training phases
  \item Surgical ablation results (accuracy impact of removing one Fourier frequency)
  \item Reward hacking simulation results
  \item Written essay connecting interpretability to alignment
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{2em}

\begin{center}
\textit{This project covers: architecture (Lessons 53--60), training dynamics (54--58),}\\
\textit{mechanistic interpretability (61, 65), singular learning theory (66),}\\
\textit{and alignment (63--64). Everything in Phase 4.}
\end{center}
